APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper directly addresses lexically constrained sequence generation for neural MT and interactive post-editing (GBS algorithm and Pick-Revise experiments, Section 4), which is squarely within top NLP/ML conference topics (NMT, decoding algorithms, sequence generation). The formulation and scope are appropriate for the community (formal GBS description, handling multi-token and discontinuous constraints, Subword Units, efficiency discussion in Sections 3.1–3.3), and the experiments use standard datasets and evaluation practices (BLEU improvements, Europarl/CommonCrawl/OpenSubtitles and an Autodesk post-editing corpus). The work is positioned with respect to relevant prior work (beam search, IMT, Bahdanau et al., Cheng et al.) and targets the right audience of NLP/ML researchers interested in decoding and interactive/domain-adaptive translation.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the practical and important problem of enforcing lexical constraints in sequence generation via Grid Beam Search (GBS), a model-agnostic decoder that “does not require any modification of the model parameters or training data.” It demonstrates strong practical impact (e.g., “an increase of over 20 BLEU points” in the Pick-Revise interactive post-editing experiments) and supports multi-token and discontinuous constraints as well as subword handling, making it broadly applicable. These properties make the method likely to be widely adopted for interactive MT, domain adaptation and other sequence tasks, and they open useful research directions for constrained generation. While highly influential and widely useful, the contribution is primarily an algorithmic/engineering advance rather than a fundamental theoretical breakthrough, so I rate its impact as strong (4).

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces Grid Beam Search (GBS), a concrete algorithm that extends standard beam search to enforce arbitrary lexical constraints ("We present Grid Beam Search (GBS) ... extends beam search to allow the inclusion of pre-specified lexical constraints") and explicitly handles multi-token and discontinuous constraints via open/closed hypotheses and coverage vectors (Sec. 3.1). It situates itself relative to prior work (noting IMT/prefix-decoding as a special case and citing Wuebker et al., Domingo et al.), clearly differentiating that GBS works with any sequence model without retraining. The techniques (grid of beams, filters for discontinuities, subword-unit integration and complexity/parallelization considerations) constitute a non-trivial and practically useful extension rather than a mere combination of known components, so the contribution is notably novel though not a paradigm shift.

CLARITY.justification: Overall organization is reasonable (sections for method, experiments, appendix) and the main contribution (Grid Beam Search for lexically constrained decoding) is stated early. However the manuscript contains repeated/duplicated paragraphs and LaTeX/reference artifacts (e.g., multiple verbatim repeats of ‘‘4 Models...’’ and broken citations like ‘‘Bahdanau et al., 2014’’ repeated), missing or referenced-but-absent items (Eq. 4, Figure 3, Tables 1/3) and some underspecified notation (coverage vector and generate/start/continue functions are described informally without full definitions). These presentation issues and grammatical/repetition problems make parts of the paper harder to follow despite generally clear high-level flow.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper presents a plausible algorithm (GBS) for lexically-constrained decoding and gives reasonable implementation details (multitoken constraints with coverage vectors; subword handling; complexity noted as O(k t c) and parallelizable). Experimental claims (e.g. >20 BLEU gains in Pick-Revise, Table 1) are promising but the provided text lacks key methodological details: no explicit comparison to alternative constrained-decoding baselines, no statistical significance testing, and the quantitative results/tables are not shown here. Mathematical material is referenced (generate/start/continue, Eq.4) but not fully included, so derivations cannot be verified from the excerpt; overall the technical approach appears sound but some experimental and evidential support is missing or insufficiently detailed.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper provides substantive experiments (interactive Pick-Revise simulations across multiple language pairs with reported >20 BLEU gains and a domain-adaptation setup using the Autodesk post-editing corpus) and supplies many training/configuration details (model architectures, vocab sizes, iterations, beamSize=10, Appendix data descriptions). However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (no empirical study of beam size, constraint types, runtime scaling, or robustness to noisy / real user constraints), and failure cases are only discussed qualitatively (efficiency complexity in §3.3 and subword mitigation in §3.2) rather than empirically. Related work appears well covered with many citations, and reproducibility is reasonably supported but missing some implementation/runtime specifics and exact preprocessing/tokenization choices.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper evaluates GBS against baseline NMT systems and a Pick-Revise (Cheng et al., 2016) simulation and reports large BLEU gains (Table 1) using consistent model/config/data details (beam size, subword setup, corpora). However, although it cites relevant prior work (IMT, prefix-decoding, Wuebker et al., Domingo et al., etc.), it does not present experimental comparisons to other constrained-decoding or interactive-MT algorithms nor acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably or better. The paper provides some analysis of efficiency and qualitative effects of constraints but lacks thorough empirical comparison and analysis against key competing approaches.
